question_title: Will the Florida Supreme Court issue any order or ruling in the litigation over Florida's 2026 mid-decade congressional map between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026?
summary: The Florida redistricting case (Equal Ground v. Byrd) is still at the trial court level as of available evidence. Judge Hawkes heard arguments on May 15 and took the temporary injunction under advisement, saying he would issue a written ruling without specifying when. For the FL Supreme Court to issue any order by May 31, the case must traverse multiple procedural steps: Hawkes must rule, an appeal must be filed, and the case must reach the FL Supreme Court through either the normal 1st DCA appellate process or an emergency/direct pathway. With only 10 days remaining and no ruling from Hawkes yet found, the timeline is very compressed. However, the June 8 qualifying deadline creates enormous urgency, multiple well-resourced parties are committed to fast appellate action, and the broad resolution criteria (any order, including denials) lower the bar once something reaches the court. Monte Carlo simulations with Bayesian updating on the absence of a Hawkes ruling suggest ~25-34% probability. I estimate 25%, reflecting genuine but below-even odds that the multi-step legal chain can complete within this tight window.

factors.description: Case is still at trial court level - Hawkes heard arguments May 15 and hasn't ruled yet (as of available news). Multiple procedural steps (trial ruling → 1st DCA → FL Supreme Court) needed before FL SC can act.

June 8 candidate qualifying deadline creates extreme urgency - all parties and courts are under pressure to move at emergency speed. WUSF says decision 'will almost certainly be appealed to the state Supreme Court.'
Broad resolution criteria - ANY order counts including denials, stays, procedural rulings. Very low bar for YES once any filing reaches the FL Supreme Court.

Multiple pathways exist to reach FL SC: normal 1st DCA appeal, emergency stay request, direct petition for discretionary review, 1st DCA pass-through certification. Well-resourced parties (Elias Law Group, SPLC, Campaign Legal Center) can pursue multiple simultaneously.
Only 10 days remain in window (May 21-31). Even with emergency pace, the full appellate chain typically takes 7-14+ days. Timeline is very compressed.
Conservative FL Supreme Court (DeSantis-appointed majority) may not feel urgency to take up challenge to DeSantis-backed map. Strategic delay benefits the state's position.

reasoning:
## Forecast: 25% probability (YES)

**Summary:** The Florida Supreme Court is unlikely but plausibly could issue an order on the 2026 congressional map challenge by May 31.

**Key reasoning:**
- 📍 **Current status:** The case is still at the trial court level. Judge Hawkes heard arguments on May 15 and hasn't ruled yet (based on available evidence as of May 21).
- ⏰ **Timeline crunch:** Only 10 days remain. The normal appellate chain (trial court → 1st DCA → FL Supreme Court) takes 7-14+ days, making completion by May 31 tight but not impossible at emergency speed.
- 🔑 **Urgency factors:** The June 8 qualifying deadline creates immense pressure. Media expects the case to reach the FL Supreme Court. Well-resourced parties (Elias Law Group, SPLC) are committed to fast appeals.
- 📋 **Broad criteria:** ANY order counts (denials, stays, procedural rulings) - so even a one-line rejection would resolve YES.
- ⚖️ **Conservative court:** The DeSantis-appointed FL Supreme Court majority has no structural incentive to rush involvement in a challenge to a DeSantis-backed map.

The main scenario for YES requires Hawkes ruling within the next few days, followed by rapid emergency appellate action reaching the FL Supreme Court by late May. Possible but below even odds.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Florida Supreme Court issue any order or ruling in the litigation over Florida's 2026 mid-decade congressional map between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43558)
[🚨 Equal Ground Education Fund et al v. Byrd et al](https://www.democracydocket.com/cases/florida-congressional-redistricting-challenge-equal-ground/)

[Plaintiffs make their case against Florida's map, but will a Judge stop its use before the Midterms?](https://floridapolitics.com/archives/796773-plaintiffs-make-their-case-against-floridas-map-but-will-a-judge-stop-its-use-before-the-midterms/)
[Judge weighs challenge to Florida’s new congressional map](https://www.wusf.org/courts-law/2026-05-15/judge-weighs-challenge-florida-new-congressional-map)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between May 17, 2026 and May 31, 2026 (inclusive, US Eastern time), the Florida Supreme Court issues any written order, opinion, ruling, or disposition (including a denial, dismissal, stay, transfer, grant of review, or order on the merits) in any case challenging or defending the validity of Florida's 2026 mid-decade congressional district map enacted during the April 2026 special session. The order must be issued by the Florida Supreme Court itself (not a lower court). The order must specifically pertain to litigation over the enacted 2026 congressional map (not earlier procedural petitions decided before May 17, 2026, and not unrelated redistricting matters). Resolution will be based on the Florida Supreme Court's official opinions/orders page at https://supremecourt.flcourts.gov/ and its docket system, or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) reporting on such an order. If no such order is issued in this window, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: "Any order or ruling" includes substantive merits decisions, procedural rulings, denials of review, transfers, stays, and emergency orders, so long as they are signed/issued by the Florida Supreme Court and arise in a case challenging or defending the April 2026 enacted congressional map. Orders by the First District Court of Appeal, the trial court (Judge Hawkes), or individual justices acting administratively do not count. If multiple qualifying orders issue in the window, only one is needed for YES. The Florida Supreme Court's March 2, 2026 ruling on the pre-enactment special-session petition does not count, as it occurred before the window and did not address the enacted map.

factors.description: Case is still at trial court level - Hawkes heard arguments May 15 and hasn't ruled yet (as of available news). Multiple procedural steps needed to reach FL Supreme Court.
June 8 candidate qualifying deadline creates extreme urgency - all parties and courts are under pressure to move fast. Media expects case to reach FL Supreme Court.
Broad resolution criteria - ANY order counts including denials, stays, procedural rulings. Low bar for YES once something reaches FL SC.
Multiple pathways exist to reach FL SC: normal appeal through 1st DCA, emergency stay, direct petition, 1st DCA certification. Parties can pursue simultaneously.
Only 10 days remain in window (May 21-31). Even with emergency pace, the full chain (Hawkes ruling → appeal → 1st DCA → FL SC) typically takes 7-14 days minimum.
Historical precedent: In 2022 FL redistricting, 1st DCA acted quickly to stay trial court ruling. But FL SC involvement in initial injunction phase was not as fast.
Conservative FL Supreme Court (DeSantis appointees) may not feel urgency to take up challenge to DeSantis-backed map. Delay structurally benefits the state.

input.anchor: Base rate for FL Supreme Court acting on redistricting within a short window: No direct base rate available. The FL Supreme Court ruled on a pre-enactment procedural petition on March 2, 2026, showing willingness to engage with redistricting issues. But the enacted map litigation is still at trial court level with multiple procedural steps needed. Starting from ~25% given the tight timeline and multiple steps needed.
input.assessment:
The core question is whether the Florida Supreme Court will be asked to act - and respond - within a 10-day window when the case is still at the trial court level. 

FOR YES (~25%):
- Extreme time pressure from June 8 qualifying deadline
- Both sides committed to immediate appeals
- Multiple pathways exist to bypass normal appellate chain (emergency stays, direct petitions, pass-through certification)
- Resolution criteria are broad - even a denial counts
- WUSF/WLRN articles say decision 'will almost certainly be appealed to the state Supreme Court'
- Hawkes may have already ruled (said 'coming days' on May 15)

AGAINST YES (~75%):
- As of May 21, no evidence Hawkes has ruled yet (6 days after hearing)
- Normal appellate chain requires: trial ruling → 1st DCA briefing → 1st DCA ruling → FL SC petition → FL SC response
- This chain typically takes 2-3 weeks minimum
- Even emergency motions take several days at each level
- The 1st DCA (not FL SC) is the normal first appellate stop
- Conservative FL SC majority has no incentive to rush involvement
- Monte Carlo simulation suggests ~25-34% probability depending on assumptions about Hawkes timing

KEY UNCERTAINTY: Whether Hawkes has already ruled. If yes, the probability jumps to ~50-60%. If no, probability drops to ~20-25%.
input.calibration_notes: The 25% estimate reflects the tight timeline constraint. Status quo (no change) clearly resolves NO. The probability could be higher if Hawkes has already ruled without my searches catching it, or lower if the appellate chain moves at normal (non-emergency) pace. The hedging check: 25% is not near 50%, so this represents a genuine directional view (more likely NO than YES) backed by the timeline analysis.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Has Hawkes already ruled? (Bayesian estimate: ~15% given no news found)
2. Will parties file directly at FL SC or follow normal 1st DCA route?
3. How fast will the FL SC respond to any filing?
4. Will the 1st DCA certify to FL SC for pass-through?
input.tool_audit:
USEFUL: research() provided comprehensive background on the litigation timeline, parties, and legal context. web_search found multiple news articles confirming no ruling from Hawkes as of May 15-17. fetch_url on Democracy Docket case page confirmed no ruling in case updates. fetch_url on WUSF article found key detail that Hawkes said he'd rule 'in writing' without specifying when.
EMPTY/LIMITED: fetch_url on FL Supreme Court homepage only returned navigation elements, not case data. Third research query on 1st DCA had no web access.
FAILURES: None - all tools functioned properly.
input.update_triggers:
1. News that Hawkes has ruled (would significantly increase probability)
2. News of any filing at the FL Supreme Court (would move probability to ~70%+)
3. News of 1st DCA emergency briefing order (would increase probability moderately)
4. If Hawkes still hasn't ruled by May 25, probability drops significantly
input.process_reflection: The scaffolding worked well for this legal/procedural question. Research agents provided good background context. Web search was essential for confirming the current status of the litigation. The Monte Carlo simulation was helpful for systematically exploring the timeline uncertainty, though the pathway probabilities required subjective judgment. One gap: I couldn't access the FL Supreme Court's docket directly to check for recent filings, which would have been the most direct evidence. A specialized court docket search tool would be valuable for legal questions like this.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Systematic Review

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are fully provided via the Metaculus question page (fetched via get_metaculus_questions). The agent correctly identifies and engages with the specific terms: ANY order/ruling by the Florida Supreme Court itself (not lower courts), in litigation over the enacted 2026 map, between May 17-31 inclusive, including denials, stays, procedural rulings. The agent repeatedly references the broad resolution criteria in its analysis and correctly notes that even a denial of review would count.

### Hallucinated Evidence — No Issues Found

**Factor 1 (Case still at trial court level):** Trace confirms via Democracy Docket case page fetch, multiple web searches, and WUSF/WLRN articles that Hawkes heard arguments May 15, took matter under advisement, and no ruling found. ✓

**Factor 2 (June 8 qualifying deadline):** Sourced from the Metaculus question background itself: "Candidate qualifying for congressional races begins June 8, 2026." The WUSF article mentions the decision "will almost certainly be appealed to the state Supreme Court." Both confirmed in trace. ✓

**Factor 3 (Broad resolution criteria):** Directly from the resolution criteria text fetched via get_metaculus_questions. ✓

**Factor 4 (Multiple pathways):** General legal knowledge about Florida appellate procedures, supported by trace discussion of 1st DCA certification, emergency petitions, etc. The research entry confirms the expected pathway "Leon County Circuit Court → Florida 1st DCA → Florida Supreme Court." ✓

**Factor 5 (10 days remaining):** Simple arithmetic from current date (May 21) to window end (May 31). The "7-14 days minimum" for the appellate chain is the agent's estimate, reasonable given the multiple steps required. ✓

**Factor 6 (Historical precedent):** Trace research confirms "the Florida 1st DCA stayed a lower court ruling against the map, allowing SB 2-C to be used in 2022 elections." The claim about FL SC timing in the initial phase is appropriately hedged with "not as fast" and confidence 0.5. ✓

**Factor 7 (Conservative FL SC):** Trace research confirms: "court has a conservative majority appointed largely by DeSantis" (from Metaculus background) and the research confirms "the reconstituted (DeSantis-appointed majority) Florida Supreme Court ultimately upheld it in 2025." ✓

### Double-Counting — Minor
Factors 5 (10 days remain, tight timeline) and Factor 1 (case still at trial court) overlap significantly — both are driven by the same underlying fact that the case hasn't progressed past Hawkes yet and the window is short. Factor 5 is essentially a restatement of Factor 1's implications with specific timing numbers. Combined effective logit is -2.5 × confidence-weighted, which may overstate the evidence. However, since both are negative (pushing toward NO) and the agent's final estimate of 25% already accounts for this via the Monte Carlo, the double-counting doesn't appear to distort the final estimate.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All logit signs are correct: negative factors point toward NO (trial court level, timeline constraints, conservative court), positive factors point toward YES (deadline urgency, broad criteria, multiple pathways).

### Contradictory Assessment — Minor Concern
The agent's thinking trace shows remarkable oscillation: 35-40% → 45% → 50% → 55% → 50% → 55% → 50% → 55% → 50% before the Monte Carlo forced more discipline. The first Monte Carlo gave 18.6% unconditionally and 14.4% conditional on Hawkes not having ruled. The second Monte Carlo gave 34.4% Bayesian after updating on no news. The final estimate of 25% sits between these two models but doesn't clearly explain why it chose this middle ground rather than anchoring on one or the other. The factor-implied probability (~23.6%) and the final estimate (25%) are well-aligned, which is good.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
The anchor is ~25% ("Starting from ~25% given the tight timeline and multiple steps needed"). The final estimate is 25%. No divergence. The anchor itself is reasonable — there's no strong external base rate available, and the agent arrived at 25% through scenario analysis. 

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is substantive and constructive: it argues Hawkes may have ALREADY ruled (based on "coming days" + 6 days elapsed), that emergency redistricting litigation moves faster than the agent models, that "any order" criterion is extremely broad, and that well-resourced parties have strong incentives to push fast. The counterargument estimates 35-45% as the alternative, which is a coherent opposing view. It also provides specific "what would change my mind" triggers with quantified impact.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence at 0.40 is appropriately low, reflecting genuine uncertainty about Hawkes timing, the speed of emergency procedures, and whether parties will pursue direct FL SC filings. The agent's thinking shows extensive oscillation (18-55%) which the 0.40 confidence reflects honestly.

### Probability Assessment — Reasonable
The agent's 25% estimate is well-supported:
- Monte Carlo 1 (conservative): 18.6% unconditional, 14.4% conditional on no Hawkes ruling yet
- Monte Carlo 2 (adjusted for urgency): 34.4% Bayesian conditional on no news
- Factor-implied: 23.6%
- Final: 25%

My independent assessment: The key question is whether the entire chain (Hawkes ruling → appeal → FL SC filing → FL SC response) can complete in 10 days when Hawkes hasn't ruled yet (as of available evidence). The timeline is genuinely tight. Even with emergency procedures:
- Hawkes rules: ~85% by May 25, ~95% by May 31 (reasonable given "coming days" + June 8 pressure)
- Appeal filed same/next day: ~95%
- Something reaches FL SC in time: Depends heavily on pathway. Normal 1st DCA route takes 7-14 days. Emergency/direct routes are plausible but uncertain.
- FL SC responds before May 31: Conditional on receiving a filing, probably 50-70% (they can deny quickly).

I estimate ~20-30% probability. The agent's 25% falls squarely in this range. The estimate appropriately reflects that the timeline is compressed but not impossible, with emergency procedures available, and the broad resolution criteria lower the bar for YES once something reaches the court.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent claims "FAILURES: None - all tools functioned properly." Ground truth: 17 calls, 0 errors (0.0%). Consistent. The third research sub-query's web tool unavailability is correctly noted as "no web access" rather than a tool failure. Agent's audit accurately describes the FL SC homepage limitation (navigation only, no case data).

### Worldview Consistency — PASS  
Three worldview research entries exist for this topic (florida-2026-congressional-redistricting, florida-supreme-court-2026-redistricting, florida-first-district-court-of-appeal). All are consistent with the factors — they confirm the case is at trial court level, Hawkes hasn't ruled, and the normal appellate pathway goes through the 1st DCA. No contradictions found.

### Summary
No significant errors found. All factual claims in the factors are grounded in the trace. The probability estimate of 25% is well-supported by both the Monte Carlo simulations and the factor analysis, and falls within my independent range of 20-30%. The agent conducted thorough research with multiple web searches and URL fetches, ran two Monte Carlo simulations with different assumptions, and performed a Bayesian update based on available evidence. Minor issues: some double-counting between Factors 1 and 5, and the thinking trace shows excessive oscillation before the Monte Carlo imposed discipline — but the final estimate is well-calibrated.
